question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 67-year-old man, who has been recovering in the hospital following
surgical repair of a contained ruptured abdominal
aortic aneurysm 10 days ago, suddenly develops severe abdominal pain. He
does not have chest pain or shortness of breath.
The patient's postoperative course had been uncomplicated until 4 days
ago, when he developed a low-grade fever. He has
been unable to tolerate a full liquid diet during the past 4 days. Medical
history is remarkable for hypertension,
hypercholesterolemia, and a myocardial infarction 6 years ago. Regular
medications include metoprolol, lovastatin, and an
81-mg aspirin daily. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for the past
40 years, and he typically drinks three to five
beers daily. Vital signs now are temperature 38.1°C (100.6°F), pulse 68/min
and regular, respirations 16/min, and blood
pressure 150/90 mm Hg. Physical examination shows bilateral cataracts.
Auscultation of the chest discloses diffuse mild
wheezes and a grade 1/6 soft systolic ejection murmur at the cardiac apex.
Palpation of the abdomen discloses mild to
moderate mid and right upper quadrant abdominal tenderness. There is no
guarding, rebound, or palpable mass. Rectal
examination shows no abnormalities. Peripheral pulses are normal.
Neurologic examination discloses no abnormalities.
Serum electrolyte concentrations are within the reference ranges.
Leukocyte count is 15,800/mm³ with mild left shift.
Urinalysis shows no abnormalities. ECG shows an old inferior myocardial
infarction. Chest x-ray shows scarring at the left
and right cardiac bases; no masses or infiltrates are present. Which of the
following is the most appropriate additional
diagnostic study?

options.D: This postoperative patient 10 days after open AAA repair presents with new

explanation:
Ultrasonography of the abdomen (D)
This postoperative patient 10 days after open AAA repair presents with new
severe abdominal pain, persistent low-grade fever, leukocytosis, intolerance of
oral intake, and focal RUQ tenderness without peritonitis. The differential in this
context includes postoperative acalculous cholecystitis, ischemic colitis, graft
infection/mycotic aneurysm, and aortoenteric fistula.[1][2][3][4]
Given the RUQ localization and systemic inflammation, acalculous cholecystitis
is a common postoperative complication after major vascular surgery and is best
evaluated initially with RUQ ultrasonography due to its rapid availability and
good diagnostic performance for gallbladder pathology; when equivocal, HIDA is
the confirmatory test.[5][6][7] While contrast-enhanced CT angiography is the
first-line modality for undifferentiated acute abdomen and for suspected
vascular or graft complications, the clinical picture here most strongly suggests
biliary pathology, making ultrasound the most appropriate next study.[1][2]
Arteriography is not an initial diagnostic test in this setting.[8] MRI is slower and
not first-line for acute RUQ pain.[5][6] Blood cultures may be obtained if sepsis is
suspected but will not localize the source and are not a substitute for imaging.
If ultrasound is nondiagnostic or suggests alternative pathology, escalate to
contrast-enhanced CT angiography of the abdomen/pelvis to evaluate for
ischemic colitis or graft-related complications.[1][2][4]
references: Vascular Graft Infections, Mycotic Aneurysms, and Endovascular

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 4-year-old girl with type 1 diabetes mellitus is brought to the emergency
department by her father because of a 4-
hour episode of restlessness, sweating, and confusion that occurred during
the night. Yesterday, he allowed her to eat
cupcakes and cotton candy at a county fair. At her bedtime that evening, he
increased her dose of subcutaneous
intermediate-acting and long-acting insulin. Her symptoms began 6 hours
later, then resolved spontaneously. After
being informed this morning of this nighttime episode, the mother insisted
the father bring the patient to the hospital.
On arrival, the patient is alert. Her vital signs are within normal limits.
Examination shows no abnormalities. Her
fingerstick blood glucose concentration is 72 mg/dL. Urinalysis is negative
for glucose and ketones. Which of the
following is the most likely explanation for this patient's nighttime
symptoms?

options.A: Hyperglycemia caused by increased glucose consumption
options.B: Hyperglycemia caused by increased glycogen metabolism
options.C: Hyperglycemia caused by insufficient exogenous insulin
options.D: Hypoglycemia caused by excess exogenous insulin
options.E: Hypoglycemia caused by excessive renal glucose loss

explanation:
The most likely explanation is nocturnal neuroglycopenic and adrenergic
symptoms from insulin-induced hypoglycemia after an excessive bedtime dose
of intermediate- and long-acting insulin. The clinical triad—restlessness,
sweating, confusion—maps to hypoglycemia; morning glucose is low-normal
(near the ADA level-1 threshold), and there is no glucosuria or ketonuria to
suggest overnight hyperglycemia or ketosis. Nocturnal hypoglycemia is common
in children with type 1 diabetes and is facilitated by blunted counterregulatory/
autonomic responses during sleep; increasing basal (intermediate/long-acting)
insulin specifically raises this risk.[1][2][3][4][5][6][7] A rebound hyperglycemic
“Somogyi effect” is uncommon per contemporary CGM data.[8] Therefore, the
best answer is: Hypoglycemia caused by excess exogenous insulin.
Practical notes:
ADA (American Diabetes Association) defines clinically significant
hypoglycemia beginning at <70 mg/dL; symptoms often begin around this
threshold and may have resolved by presentation.[1]
Prevention includes careful basal dosing, bedtime carbohydrate
assessment, and use of CGM/low-glucose suspend features where
available.[2][3][5][6][7]
The table below outlines basal insulin pharmacodynamics relevant to nocturnal
hypoglycemia risk; intermediate-acting NPH has a peak and greater nocturnal
hypoglycemia risk compared with flatter long-acting analogs.[6]
This visual shows the onset/peak/duration of intermediate- and long-acting
insulins, contextualizing why excess bedtime dosing predisposes to overnight
hypoglycemia.
• 
• 
Citations: ADA Standards of Care (2025) for hypoglycemia thresholds and
symptoms; ADA pediatric position statement for management and treatment
thresholds; pediatric CGM studies documenting frequent nocturnal
hypoglycemia; classic pathophysiology and sleep-related autonomic failure;
pharmacology of basal insulins; rarity of Somogyi phenomenon.[1][2][3][4][5][6]
[7][8]
references: Glycemic Goals and Hypoglycemia: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2025. .

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 22-year-old primigravid woman at 10 weeks’ gestation comes to the
emergency department (ED) because of a 3-
day history of persistent vomiting. She is treated with intravenous
hydration, pyridoxine, and doxylamine. As the
resident physician prepares the patient for discharge, he asks who will be
picking her up from the ED. She says her
husband will be arriving soon and asks if the hospital staff can refer to her
symptoms as a “stomach flu.” She does
not want anyone to mention to her husband that she is pregnant because

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
Five days after admission to the hospital for management of an ST-
elevation myocardial infarction, a 59-year-old
man has the sudden onset of severe abdominal and bilateral lower
extremity pain, cyanosis of the lower body, and
paresthesias. On hospital days 1 and 2, he received vasopressor and
inotropic therapy. On hospital day 2,
echocardiography showed extensive anterolateral akinesis of the left
ventricle. He has no history of serious illness.
His medications are metoprolol, lisinopril, simvastatin, and dalteparin. He
appears to be in acute distress. Pulse is
105/min and regular, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 80/60
mm Hg. Examination shows lower body
cyanosis to the level of the costal margins. Crackles are heard over the
lower lung fields bilaterally. Distant heart
sounds but no murmurs are heard. The abdomen is nondistended and
nontender. Pulses are 1+ in the upper
extremities and absent in the lower extremities. Muscle strength is 3/5 in
the lower extremities. Sensation to touch
and temperature is decreased over the lower extremities. ECG shows sinus
tachycardia. Which of the following is
the most likely explanation for this patient’s clinical findings?
explanation:
B. Aortic embolism
Presentation is most consistent with an acute aortic saddle embolus causing
complete aortoiliac occlusion: sudden severe abdominal and bilateral leg pain,
lower body cyanosis to the costal margin, absent lower-extremity pulses with
preserved upper-extremity pulses, and bilateral motor/sensory deficits. The
timing (day 5 after a large anterior STEMI with extensive LV akinesis) strongly
implicates embolization from a left ventricular mural thrombus, a recognized
complication after anterior MI that can lead to systemic embolization including
aortic saddle embolus.[1][2] Acute aortic occlusion typically presents with abrupt
bilateral limb ischemia, neurologic deficits from spinal cord or peripheral
ischemia, and shock if extensive territory is affected.[3]
Alternatives are less likely:
Aortic dissection usually presents with chest/back pain; malperfusion can
cause limb ischemia, but the symmetric lower-body-only findings with
preserved upper pulses and periumbilical cyanosis are classic for aortic
saddle embolus rather than dissection.[3][4]
Pericardial tamponade would produce hypotension with JVD and clear
lungs; diffuse pulse loss is not expected, and localized lower-body ischemia
with absent femoral pulses is not typical.[5][6]
Right ventricular failure causes hypotension and JVD with clear lungs, not
abrupt bilateral lower limb ischemia or absent lower pulses.
Ventricular free-wall rupture leads to hemopericardium and tamponade
physiology rather than isolated lower-body ischemia.[6]
Vasopressors can precipitate peripheral limb ischemia, but they do not explain
the abrupt, symmetric aortoiliac-level ischemia with neurologic deficits and
• 
• 
• 
• 
preserved upper extremity perfusion; the post-MI LV thrombus mechanism is
more consistent.[1][3][7]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 31-year-old woman, gravida 3, para 2, who is at 32 weeks' gestation, is
admitted to the hospital because of a 1-week history
of progressive dyspnea and wheezing. She says her heart is "racing" and she
is coughing up a small amount of blood-streaked
sputum. Medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she
takes levothyroxine. An intravenous catheter is
placed. Vital signs are temperature 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse 132/min,
respirations 32/min, and blood pressure 135/78 mm Hg.
Pulse oximetry on 100% oxygen via nasal cannula shows an oxygen
saturation of 92%. Auscultation of the lungs discloses
decreased breath sounds at the bases with expiratory crackles bilaterally.
Cardiac examination discloses an irregularly
irregular rhythm, an indistinct point of maximal impulse, and a loud S1. A
grade 3/6, low-pitched, diastolic, rumbling
murmur is audible at the apex; a distinct snapping sound precedes the
murmur. Fetal heart rate is 144/min. Which of the
following is the most likely cause of the findings in this patient?

options.A: Atrial septal defect with development of pulmonary hypertension
options.B: Chronic mitral regurgitation secondary to rheumatic heart disease
options.E: Mitral stenosis complicated by atrial fibrillation

explanation:
Mitral stenosis complicated by atrial fibrillation
The presentation is classic for severe rheumatic mitral stenosis decompensating
in late pregnancy: loud S1, diastolic low-pitched rumble at the apex with an
opening snap, irregularly irregular rhythm (atrial fibrillation), pulmonary edema
(crackles, hypoxemia despite oxygen), and hemoptysis. Pregnancy-induced
increases in blood volume and heart rate exacerbate transmitral gradients and
left atrial pressure, precipitating pulmonary venous hypertension, atrial
arrhythmias, and heart failure in previously compensated MS.[1][2][3][4] The
American Heart Association emphasizes left-sided stenotic lesions—especially
rheumatic MS—as among the highest-risk valvular pathologies in pregnancy,
with AF and pulmonary edema as typical complications.[1][4] Echocardiography-
based pathophysiology in rheumatic MS explains dyspnea and hemoptysis via
elevated left atrial and pulmonary venous pressures, worsened by tachycardia/
AF that shortens diastolic filling time.[2]
Alternatives are less likely:
ASD with pulmonary hypertension typically causes right-sided chamber
enlargement/murmurs rather than a classic apical diastolic rumble with
opening snap; pregnancy is generally tolerated without severe hypoxemia
unless advanced pulmonary vascular disease is present.[5][6][7][8]
Chronic MR produces a holosystolic apical murmur and is relatively better
tolerated in pregnancy compared with stenosis.[1][3]
Coarctation and congenital aortic stenosis do not explain the apical
diastolic rumble with opening snap or hemoptysis/pulmonary edema
pattern.
This scenario aligns most closely with severe rheumatic MS with AF in
pregnancy, a high-risk state associated with heart failure and maternal
morbidity.[1][3][4]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A male newborn is delivered at term to a 28-year-old primigravid woman.
Physical examination shows a broad, flat
nose; low-set ears; a receding chin; prominent infraorbital folds; and
bilateral abdominal masses. The newborn
develops cyanosis, difficulty breathing, and polyuria and dies 24 hours after
birth. At autopsy, examination shows
pulmonary hypoplasia and enlarged, polycystic kidneys. Genetic testing
shows two copies of a gene having a
pathogenic mutation with a disease incidence rate in the general
population of 1 in 10,000. Which of the following
best describes the mutation occurrence rate for this newborn's disease?

options.A: Carrier frequency (2pq): ~1/50, consistent with rare-allele approximation (p

explanation:
The presentation is classic for autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease
(ARPKD) due to biallelic PKHD1 mutations, with perinatal lethal disease
characterized by enlarged echogenic kidneys, oligohydramnios/Potter sequence,
and pulmonary hypoplasia.[1][2][3][4][5] For an autosomal recessive disease,
incidence ≈ q² under Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium. Given incidence = 1/10,000, q
= √(1/10,000) = 1/100. Thus, the mutant allele frequency (mutation occurrence
rate) is 1/100, and the heterozygote (carrier) frequency is approximately 2pq ≈
2(1)(1/100) ≈ 1/50.[6][7][8]
Therefore, the correct choice is: 1/100.
Rationale:
Disease incidence (q²): 1/10,000
Mutant allele frequency (q): 1/100 → option (A)
Carrier frequency (2pq): ~1/50, consistent with rare-allele approximation (p
≈ 1).[6][7][8]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 75-year-old man comes to the clinic because of a 2-year history of
progressive shortness of breath. He also has
had an occasional dry cough for the past 6 months. He has not had chest
pain, palpitations, or swelling of his legs.
Medical history is unremarkable, and the patient takes no medications. He
has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily
for 57 years. He does not drink alcoholic beverages. Vital signs are
temperature 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse 92/min,
respirations 18/min, and blood pressure 122/76 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on
room air shows an oxygen saturation of
89%. The patient has mild dyspnea when he speaks. Auscultation of the
lungs discloses fine and dry crackles in the
bases of both lungs. Cardiac examination discloses a normal S1 and S2. No
extra heart sounds are heard. Jugular
venous pulsation is measured at 2 cm above the sternal angle with the head
of the bed elevated at 30 degrees.
Examination of the extremities shows clubbing of the digits of both hands.
Results of complete blood count, serum
chemistry profile, and urinalysis are within the reference ranges. Chest x-
ray shows scattered reticular opacities in
both lung bases. There is no hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following
is the most appropriate next step in
evaluation?

options.B: High-resolution computed tomography scan of the chest
options.D: Serum antiglomerular basement membrane antibody assay

explanation:
The presentation (older man, progressive exertional dyspnea, chronic dry cough,
bibasilar “Velcro” crackles, clubbing, hypoxemia, basilar reticular opacities on
CXR) is classic for interstitial lung disease, particularly idiopathic pulmonary
fibrosis. High-resolution CT is the key next diagnostic step to establish the
presence and pattern of fibrosis (e.g., UIP vs alternatives), which often obviates
the need for biopsy and guides subsequent testing and management. The
American Thoracic Society/European Respiratory Society/Japanese Respiratory
Society/Latin American Thoracic Association guideline explicitly requires HRCT
in suspected IPF, and thoracic CT has superior sensitivity and utility versus chest
radiography for ILD subtyping.[1][2][3][4] Biopsy is reserved for cases with non-
diagnostic or discordant HRCT findings, and carries higher risk in older
hypoxemic patients.[5][6] Echocardiography, serologies, and PET do not replace
HRCT as the first imaging step in this context.[3][7][8][9][10]
Answer: (B) High-resolution computed tomography scan of the chest

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 70-year-old man is admitted to the hospital through the emergency
department because of a 12-hour history of severe
nausea and four episodes of vomiting of undigested food. During the past
week, he has had worsening nausea, heartburn
following meals, and early satiety. Medical history is significant for type 2
diabetes mellitus diagnosed 20 years ago,
hyperlipidemia, and hypertension. Medications are intermediate-acting
insulin twice daily and insulin lispro before meals. He
also takes enalapril and atorvastatin. On admission, the patient appears
uncomfortable. Vital signs are temperature 36.2°C
(97.1°F), pulse 102/min, respirations 16/min, and blood pressure 105/78 mm
Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an
oxygen saturation of 98%. Physical examination discloses mild epigastric
tenderness to palpation. The remainder of the
examination discloses no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies
ordered in the emergency department are shown:
Serum Blood ALT 23 U/L
AST 26 U/L
Alkaline phosphatase 85 U/L
Amylase 104 U/L
Urea nitrogen 42 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.32 mg/dL
Hemoglobin A1c 8.2%
X-rays of the chest and abdomen disclose no abnormalities. Which of the
following will best explain the patient's condition?

options.A: Describes choledocholithiasis/pigment or mixed gallstones; this does
options.C: Decreased gastric pH is not a primary mechanism of gastroparesis and
options.D: Increased cholecystokinin inhibits gastric emptying by reducing antral

explanation:
The best explanation is Decreased gastric myoelectrical activity (B).
This presentation is classic for diabetic gastroparesis: subacute worsening of
early satiety, postprandial fullness/heartburn, and vomiting of undigested food
in a patient with long-standing diabetes and no mechanical obstruction on
imaging. Gastroparesis is defined as delayed gastric emptying without
obstruction and is strongly associated with these symptoms and poor glycemic
control.[1][2][3][4][5] The American College of Gastroenterology and the
American Diabetes Association emphasize diagnosis with gastric emptying
studies after excluding obstruction.[1][6]
Pathophysiology in diabetes centers on gastric neuromuscular dysfunction with
impaired myoelectrical activity and antral hypomotility due to autonomic (vagal)
neuropathy, injury/dysfunction of interstitial cells of Cajal, and smooth muscle
abnormalities, often exacerbated by hyperglycemia; these mechanisms slow
gastric emptying and produce the symptom constellation described.[2][3][4][7]
Why other options are incorrect:
(A) Describes choledocholithiasis/pigment or mixed gallstones; this does
not explain vomiting of undigested food and early satiety with normal
radiographs.[5]
(C) Decreased gastric pH is not a primary mechanism of gastroparesis and
would not account for delayed emptying.[3][4]
(D) Increased cholecystokinin inhibits gastric emptying by reducing antral
motility, but the patient’s syndrome is best attributed to diabetes-related
• 
• 
• 
neuromuscular dysfunction rather than isolated hormonal excess;
moreover, CCK’s effect is inhibitory rather than causative of the underlying
neuropathic disorder.[8][9][10]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A previously healthy 20-year-old woman, who is a sophomore in college, is
brought to the emergency department
by her roommates because of an 8-hour history of weakness and vomiting
blood and a 2-day history of dizziness.
She has no history of similar symptoms. She says she overeats in response
to the stress of her classes. She induces
vomiting after consuming a significant amount of food because she feels
"too full" and "out of control." She now
vomits almost daily. She feels she is slightly overweight. She is physically
active and exercises moderately two to
three times weekly. She does not use laxatives or diuretics. She has many
friends and enjoys going out socially. She
takes no medications. She drinks three to four beers on weekends and does
not use illicit drugs. She appears
uncomfortable and is tearful and mildly diaphoretic. She is 157 cm (5 ft 2 in)
tall and weighs 59 kg (130 lb); BMI is
24 kg/m². Temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse is 115/min and regular, and
blood pressure is 100/68 mm Hg.
Examination shows tender parotid glands bilaterally and poor dentition.
Results of laboratory studies are most likely
to show which of the following in this patient?

options.A: K+ is Decreased, Cl⁻ is decreased, HCO³⁻ is decreased
options.B: K+ is Decreased, Cl⁻ is decreased,, HCO³⁻ is increased
options.C: K+ is Decreased, Cl⁻ is increased, HCO³⁻ is decreased
options.D: K+ is Increased, Cl⁻ is decreased, HCO³⁻ is increased
options.E: K+ is Increased, Cl⁻ is increased, HCO³⁻ is decreased

explanation:
The clinical picture is consistent with bulimia nervosa with frequent self-induced
vomiting (parotid enlargement, dental erosion). Gastric HCl loss produces a
chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis with renal maintenance via RAAS
activation and distal H+/K+ secretion, yielding hypokalemia, hypochloremia, and
elevated bicarbonate. Therefore, the most likely laboratory pattern is: Potassium
is decreased, chloride is decreased, bicarbonate is increased (B). This pattern is
described across pediatric and adult guidelines and corroborated by clinical
studies and acid–base reviews.[1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8]
Context for visual: The NEJM physiologic framework separates chloride-
responsive metabolic alkalosis from chloride-resistant causes and explains how
volume depletion, hypokalemia, and hypochloremia maintain alkalemia after
vomiting—precisely the mechanism in purging bulimia.[6][8]
Key pathophysiology and clinical correlations:
Loss of gastric HCl increases serum HCO3−; volume and Cl− depletion
impair bicarbonate excretion; aldosterone-driven distal H+ and K+
secretion sustains alkalosis and hypokalemia.[6][7][8]
Hypokalemia and hypochloremia are frequently observed in bulimia
nervosa and correlate with purging frequency.[2][3][5][9]
Management is isotonic saline with KCl repletion to correct ECF volume,
chloride deficit, and potassium deficit; this reverses the alkalosis by
restoring renal bicarbonate excretion.[6][7][8]
• 
• 
• 
Safety considerations: monitor for arrhythmias with hypokalemia; correct
electrolytes cautiously and address ongoing purging behaviors per American
Psychiatric Association guidance.[2]

(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 70-year-old woman comes to the office for an annual health maintenance
examination. She describes a 1-year history of
slowly progressive fatigue, diffuse muscle aches, and generalized pain.
Medical history is significant for osteoporosis
diagnosed 5 years ago. Medications include alendronate and calcium
supplementation. Vital signs are normal. Physical
examination is unremarkable for her age, with no point tenderness on
palpation. Results of complete blood count, serum
electrolyte and thyroid-stimulating hormone concentrations, liver function
tests, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate are all
within the reference ranges. Screening mammography and colonoscopy
done 1 year ago disclosed no abnormalities. Which
of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?

options.E: . Vitamin D deficiency is prevalent in older adults, can present with

explanation:
The most appropriate next step is to obtain a serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D assay
(E). Vitamin D deficiency is prevalent in older adults, can present with
nonspecific fatigue and diffuse myalgias, and is particularly relevant in a patient
with osteoporosis on bisphosphonate therapy; guidelines from the American
Association of Clinical Endocrinology recommend measuring 25(OH)D as part of
the laboratory evaluation in women with osteoporosis, and major reviews
emphasize checking 25(OH)D in those with bone disease and musculoskeletal
symptoms.[1][2][3][4][5] Low 25(OH)D may contribute to osteomalacic pain and
suboptimal response to alendronate; repletion is indicated if deficient.[3][5]
Other options are lower yield here: repeat DXA is not necessary for symptom
evaluation; EMG/NCS and cervical MRI are not indicated without focal
neurologic deficits; ANA is low yield with normal exam and ESR; “no further
evaluation” risks missing a common, treatable cause.[6][7][8][9]
If deficient, typical repletion regimens (per Endocrine-focused guidance) use
cholecalciferol to achieve ≥20–30 ng/mL, with many osteoporosis guidelines
targeting ≥30 ng/mL; dosing should be individualized to baseline level and
comorbidities.[4][6]

the evaluation of postmenopausal osteoporosis and consideration of
osteomalacia among alternative diagnoses.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A previously healthy 64-year-old man is brought to the emergency
department 3 hours after the sudden onset of severe
flank pain. He has no history of similar pain or serious illness. He takes no
medications. He appears to be in distress.
His pulse is 100/min, and blood pressure is 168/92 mm Hg. Physical
examination shows a soft abdomen with left sided tenderness. Urinalysis
shows microscopic hematuria. A CT scan of the abdomen shows a small
ureteric calculus.
Analgesic therapy is initiated and the pain resolves 1 hour later. The urine is
then strained and a uric acid calculus is
found. Which of the following processes is most likely impaired in this
patient?
explanation:
The most likely impaired process in this patient is: Purine metabolism.
Rationale:
Uric acid is the final product of purine catabolism in humans; uric acid
nephrolithiasis reflects abnormalities in purine handling (overproduction
and/or underexcretion of urate), with precipitation favored by persistently
low urine pH and supersaturation. Thus, a uric acid ureteral stone directly
implicates purine metabolism rather than bile salts, cholesterol,
cytochrome P450, or the urea cycle.[1][2][3][4][5][6]
Pathophysiology centers on increased uric acid load and unduly acidic
urine (pH < pKa ≈ 5.5), which titrates urate to poorly soluble uric acid and
promotes crystallization. Renal transporters and ammoniagenesis defects
that acidify urine are common contributors, particularly in metabolic
syndrome and gout, but the biochemical source of uric acid remains purine
breakdown.[1][3][4][5][6]
Clinical guidance emphasizes evaluating and managing uric acid stones by
addressing urate burden and urine pH (e.g., alkalinization), consistent with
a disturbance in purine metabolism rather than other listed pathways. The
American Urological Association recommends 24-hour urine testing
including uric acid and pH to inform prevention strategies. The American
College of Rheumatology notes that xanthine oxidase inhibition (e.g.,
allopurinol or febuxostat) lowers urinary uric acid in patients with
urolithiasis, further linking stone risk to purine metabolism.[7][8]
Incorrect options:
Bile salt and cholesterol metabolism relate to gallstones, not uric acid
nephrolithiasis.
Cytochrome P450 activity is not mechanistically linked to uric acid stone
formation.
Urea cycle defects cause hyperammonemia, not uric acid urolithiasis.

(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 58-year-old man comes to the clinic for a follow-up examination 3
months after beginning warfarin therapy for
deep venous thrombosis. He noted blood in his urine 1 week ago but has
not seen any since that time. He has not had
pain, fever, or increased urinary frequency. He has not sustained any recent
trauma. His prothrombin time and INR
have been within the therapeutic ranges during his previous
measurements. He has hypertension and benign
prostatic hyperplasia. Medications are warfarin and hydrochlorothiazide.
He smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for
40 years but quit when diagnosed with deep venous thrombosis.
Temperature is 35.9°C (96.6°F), pulse is 88/min,
respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 135/87 mm Hg. The prostate
is moderately enlarged; no nodules are
noted.
Laboratory studies showed that the Prothrombin time is 19 sec (INR=2.5),
serum Urea nitrogen is 18 mg/dL, serum Creatinine is 1 mg/dL, and
Prostate-specific antigen is 3.8 ng/mL (N<4).
The laboratory result on urine showed the following: blood is 3+, protein is
1+, RBC is too numerous to count, WBC is 2/hpf, no casts, and negative for
Nitrites and Leukocyte esterase.
In addition to a CT scan of the abdomen, which of the following is the most
appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Adjustment of the warfarin dosage to maintain an INR between 1.5 and

explanation:
Cystoscopy is the most appropriate next step because guidelines and evidence
indicate that all patients with gross hematuria and risk factors—including those
on therapeutic anticoagulation—require prompt urologic evaluation to exclude
malignancy. The patient has gross hematuria, is male, age >35, with a heavy
smoking history; anticoagulation at a therapeutic INR is not considered a
sufficient explanation, and malignancy must be ruled out with cystoscopy plus
upper tract imaging.[1][2][3][4] The American Urological Association (AUA/SUFU)
also recommends cystoscopy as part of risk-based evaluation for hematuria; the
American College of Physicians emphasizes that antithrombotic therapy should
not preclude evaluation.[4][5] Observational data show antithrombotic therapy
can unmask otherwise silent bladder cancers, reinforcing the need for
cystoscopy despite therapeutic INR.[3] Empiric antibiotics are not indicated
without infection, finasteride is a consideration only after exclusion of
malignancy, hydrochlorothiazide is not causally implicated, and down-titration
of warfarin is inappropriate with therapeutic INR.[1][2][6][7]
Therefore, in addition to CT imaging of the upper tracts, the next step is 
Cystoscopy.
To contextualize the diagnostic approach, comparative modeling of guideline
strategies shows that cystoscopy is a core test across risk-based pathways for
hematuria; CT urography is generally reserved for higher-risk presentations
such as gross hematuria or smokers, aligning with this patient’s profile.[8]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 22-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 4‐day
history of cough productive of green sputum as well as fever, chills, and
rigors. He also has had a 3‐day history of progressive shortness of breath;
he now has shortness of breath at rest. He was evaluated in an urgent care
center 1 day ago and was prescribed azithromycin, but his symptoms have
worsened. He is using accessory muscles of respiration. He is diaphoretic
and is able to give only one- to two-word answers to questions. Vital signs
are temperature 39.1°C (102.3°F), pulse 138/min, respirations 40/min, and
blood pressure 103/56 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an
oxygen saturation of 80%, and 100% oxygen is administered via
nonrebreather face mask. Skin is mottled and there is cyanosis of the
fingers and toes. Pupils measure 4 mm in diameter and are reactive to light.
Mucous membranes are dry. Auscultation of the lungs discloses bilateral
crackles. The remainder of the physical examination discloses no
abnormalities. Laboratory studies are ordered; results of arterial blood gas
analysis on 100% oxygen via nonrebreather face mask are shown:
PO2: 50 mm Hg
PCO2: 44 mm Hg
pH: 7.34
Oxygen saturation: 85%
Chest x-ray shows bilateral infiltrates with a normal cardiac silhouette and
no effusions.
Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's
condition?
explanation:
Ventilation-perfusion mismatch and shunt (D)
The presentation is consistent with severe acute hypoxemic respiratory failure
from pneumonia complicated by ARDS: acute onset, bilateral infiltrates with
normal cardiac silhouette, profound hypoxemia refractory to 100% O2 (PaO2 50
mm Hg; SaO2 85% on NRB), tachypnea with near-normal PaCO2, and no signs of
cardiogenic edema. In ARDS, the predominant mechanisms of hypoxemia are
true intrapulmonary shunt (perfused, nonventilated alveoli from alveolar
flooding/collapse) and extensive low V/Q regions; these are characteristically
poorly responsive to increased FiO2, unlike hypoventilation, obstructive disease,
or isolated diffusion limitation.[1][2][3][4][5][6]
Key points:
Shunt/low V/Q cause large A–a gradients and refractory hypoxemia; near-
normal PaCO2 is common early due to high minute ventilation.[2][3][4]
Breathing 100% O2 often fails to normalize PaO2 in shunt-dominant states
and may worsen atelectasis in acute lung injury, further increasing shunt.
[5]
Hypoventilation would produce hypercapnia with alveolar hypoxemia that
corrects with oxygen; obstructive disease alone would not explain bilateral
infiltrates and such severe refractory hypoxemia; pure diffusion limitation
rarely causes this degree of hypoxemia at rest and typically improves with
high FiO2.[2][3][6][7]
• 
• 
• 
Overall, the gas exchange pattern and imaging most strongly indicate shunt-
dominant V/Q mismatch as seen in ARDS secondary to severe pneumonia.[1][3]
[4][5][6]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 32-year-old woman with hypothyroidism comes to the physician because
of a 6-week history of cold intolerance, constipation, and severe fatigue.
She also has had a 4.5-kg (10-lb) weight gain during this period. Her current
medication is daily levothyroxine. She also started taking calcium
carbonate supplements 2 months ago for osteopenia of the lumbar spine.
At that time, serum concentrations of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH)
and free thyroxine (FT4) were within the reference ranges. Today, physical
examination shows coarse, dry skin. Serum studies show a TSH
concentration of 16.6 μU/mL and FT4 concentration of 0.3 ng/dL. Which of
the following pharmacokinetic changes of levothyroxine is the most likely
cause of this patient’s current condition?
explanation:
The correct choice is: Decreased absorption.
Calcium carbonate forms insoluble complexes with levothyroxine in the acidic
gastric milieu, reducing its bioavailability and causing a rise in TSH with a fall in
free T4 after coadministration—consistent with this patient’s course. The
American Thyroid Association (US) notes calcium salts reduce levothyroxine
Cmax and AUC in absorption studies, and clinical data show TSH rises during
coadministration and normalizes after separation/discontinuation.[1][2][3] FDA
labeling for multiple levothyroxine products instructs administering
levothyroxine at least 4 hours apart from calcium carbonate to avoid reduced
absorption.[4][5][6][7]
According to a review published in The New England Journal of Medicine:
"Drugs interfering with gastrointestinal absorption of thyroid
hormone include ferrous sulfate, calcium carbonate, aluminum
hydroxide, sucralfate, bile acid sequestrants, and raloxifene."
—Henry B. Burch, M.D., National Institutes of Health. Drug Effects on
the Thyroid. N Engl J Med. August 22, 2019.
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
Practical management:
Separate levothyroxine from calcium supplements by ≥4 hours; take
levothyroxine fasting, 30–60 minutes before breakfast or at bedtime (3–4
hours after the last meal).[1][4][5][6][7][8]
Recheck TSH and free T4 6–8 weeks after changing timing or stopping
calcium to confirm restoration of euthyroidism.[1]
If separation is not feasible, consider a modest levothyroxine dose increase
with close monitoring, or a liquid/soft-gel levothyroxine formulation that
may be less affected by gastric pH/complexation.[1][8]
This interaction affects the absorption phase; there is no evidence here for
altered distribution, metabolism, or elimination, and the effect is reversible with
proper timing or discontinuation.[1][2][3]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 32-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department
immediately after being involved in a motor vehicle
collision. She was the unrestrained driver. On arrival, she says she has
severe pain in her right groin. On
examination, any movement of the right hip produces pain. An x-ray of the
pelvis shows a completely displaced
fracture of the femoral neck. In addition to intravenous morphine, which of
the following is the most appropriate
management?

options.D: Limited weight bearing with use of crutches

explanation:
Operative fixation is indicated. In a young adult with a completely displaced
femoral neck fracture from high-energy trauma, urgent anatomical reduction
and stable internal fixation is the standard to preserve the femoral head and
mitigate risks of osteonecrosis and nonunion; nonoperative options (bed rest,
traction, bracing, limited weight bearing) are inadequate and associated with
poor outcomes.[1][2][3] Contemporary evidence in this age group favors fixed-
angle constructs over multiple cancellous screws alone for displaced patterns,
with augmentation (e.g., sliding hip screw plus medial femoral neck buttress
plate and antirotation screw) improving construct success; surgery should occur
within 24–48 hours when feasible.[1][3][4][5][6]
Correct answer: Operative reduction and internal fixation.
Figure 4 from NEJM illustrates the treatment schema: internal fixation for
femoral-neck fractures and arthroplasty options for displaced fractures in older
adults; for young patients with displaced femoral-neck fractures, the goal is head
preservation via reduction and internal fixation rather than arthroplasty.[1] This
visual reinforces that conservative measures are not definitive care for this injury
pattern.
Figure 4. Selected Treatments for Hip Fracture.
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
Why not the other options:
Bed rest, traction, hip spica, or limited weight bearing do not address
vascular compromise or instability and carry high risks of nonunion and
avascular necrosis in displaced femoral-neck fractures.[1][2][5][6]
Closed reduction followed by traction is obsolete for definitive care;
definitive internal fixation is required.[1][2][3]
• 
• 
Key operative considerations:
Urgent closed or open reduction to restore alignment and decompress
intracapsular pressure; stable internal fixation with a fixed-angle construct,
often augmented with a medial buttress plate and antirotation screw, has
lower failure than screws alone in displaced fractures in younger adults.[4]
[5]
Early surgery within 24–48 hours optimizes outcomes.[1][3]
references: Modern Methods of Treating Hip Fractures. Parker MJ, Gurusamy K..

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 33-year-old male physician reports for a shift in the emergency
department. A nurse alerts you that he noticed a faint odor
of alcohol near the physician. When approached, the physician appears
tired and more disheveled than usual. There is an
odor of alcohol on his breath. He is a skilled and talented physician with no
known history of substance or alcohol use
disorder. He is married with three children, and his wife is pregnant with
twins. The physician was recently hired and has had
no actions on his license by any state medical board. Which of the
following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Self-report is unreliable and does not satisfy reporting obligations;
options.B: Colleague-initiated BAC testing is not the first step; institutions have
options.C: Relieve the physician of duty and alert the hospital's patient safety officer
options.D: Informal coverage without reporting circumvents required safety
options.E: Sending home without formal notification fails ethical/legal duties and

explanation:
Relieve the physician of duty and alert the hospital's patient safety officer (C).
Suspected on-duty impairment requires immediate removal from clinical duties
and notification through institutional safety channels to protect patients and
initiate appropriate evaluation. Ethical and, in many jurisdictions, legal duties
obligate physicians to report potentially impaired colleagues; reliance on self-
attestation, informal coverage, or sending the colleague home without formal
reporting fails to meet these obligations and exposes patients and the institution
to risk.[1][2][3][4] Immediate reporting also aligns with Joint Commission–driven
safety programs and just culture processes that ensure nonpunitive pathways for
assessment, treatment, and, when appropriate, monitored return to practice.[5]
[6]
Why other options are inappropriate:
(A) Self-report is unreliable and does not satisfy reporting obligations;
patient safety remains at risk.[1][2][4]
(B) Colleague-initiated BAC testing is not the first step; institutions have
defined protocols via occupational health/employee health and patient
safety channels.[3][5]
(D) Informal coverage without reporting circumvents required safety
processes and documentation.[4][5]
(E) Sending home without formal notification fails ethical/legal duties and
delays proper evaluation and support.[2][4][5]
This approach prioritizes patient safety while enabling a nonpunitive evaluation
and access to physician health resources and rehabilitation pathways.[2][3][6]
references: Physician Impairment and Rehabilitation: Reintegration Into Medical
A Call to Restore Your Calling: Self-Care of the Emergency Physician in the
Impaired Healthcare Professional. Baldisseri MR.. Critical Care Medicine.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 57-year-old man comes to the physician 1 week after he had a 45-minute
episode of slurred speech and
clumsiness of his right hand. The episode occurred during his daily walk;
the symptoms resolved during the next 24
hours. He has no history of similar symptoms. He has a 20-year history of
hypertension and a 15-year history of
hypercholesterolemia controlled with a low-cholesterol diet and daily
exercise. He takes no medications. He has
smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 40 years and drinks one to two
glasses of wine nightly with dinner. He is
alert and fully oriented. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 86 kg (190
lb); BMI is 27 kg/m². His temperature is
37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 88/min, respirations are 10/min, and blood
pressure is 170/90 mm Hg. Examination,
including neurologic examination, shows no other abnormalities. Daily
enteric-coated aspirin therapy is begun.
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to decrease this
patient’s risk for subsequent cerebral
infarction?
explanation:
Antihypertensive therapy.
Rationale: After TIA, blood pressure lowering provides the largest proven
reduction in recurrent stroke risk. The American College of Cardiology/
American Heart Association recommend initiating or intensifying
antihypertensive therapy a few days after TIA when BP is ≥140/90 mm Hg, with
preferred agents including a thiazide diuretic, ACE inhibitor, ARB, or a thiazide–
ACE inhibitor combination; a target <130/80 mm Hg is reasonable for most
patients.[1][2] Trials summarized by the American Stroke Association show BP
lowering after stroke/TIA reduces recurrence by roughly 30–40% (e.g.,
indapamide alone; perindopril ± indapamide) and support prioritizing these
classes.[3] This patient’s BP (170/90 mm Hg) is markedly above target, making
antihypertensive therapy the most immediate and impactful next step.
Other measures:
High-intensity statin therapy is recommended for presumed
atherosclerotic TIA and reduces recurrent vascular events, but BP control
has stronger direct evidence for recurrent stroke reduction and is the most
urgent abnormality here.[2][3][4]
Smoking cessation improves outcomes after stroke/TIA and should be
counseled, but effect sizes in observational data are smaller and less
immediate than BP lowering.[3][5]
Alcohol reduction and weight optimization are advisable for risk factor
control, but evidence for preventing recurrent stroke events is less direct.
[3][4]
Agent selection and dosing should be individualized; commonly used regimens
include a thiazide (e.g., chlorthalidone 12.5–25 mg daily) and/or ACE inhibitor
• 
• 
• 
(e.g., perindopril 4–8 mg daily) or ARB, titrated to achieve <130/80 mm Hg as
tolerated.[1][2][3]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 60-year-old man comes to the office because of a 1-year history of
progressive left groin pain. The pain is worse with
exercise and is relieved with rest. He says the pain is beginning to affect his
ability to carry out his daily activities. He says,
"It even hurts when I'm turning over in bed." He does not recall any trauma
to the area. He also has had generalized mild
stiffness of his joints during the past 3 years. He takes atorvastatin for
hyperlipidemia and lisinopril for hypertension.
Medical history is otherwise unremarkable. He does not smoke cigarettes.
He drinks one alcoholic cocktail daily. He is 178
cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 68 kg (150 lb); BMI is 22 kg/m². Vital signs are
temperature 37.4°C (99.3°F), pulse 82/min,
respirations 18/min, and blood pressure 120/78 mm Hg. The patient walks
with a slight limp and has difficulty changing
position. Abduction of the left hip elicits pain. Physical examination of the
patient's other joints discloses no abnormalities.
Which of the following is the most appropriate imaging study at this time?
explanation:
Bold answer: Plain x-ray of the left hip (D).
Rationale: The presentation—chronic, progressive anterior groin pain
exacerbated by activity with mechanical features and pain on hip abduction—
suggests intra-articular pathology, most likely osteoarthritis. For chronic hip
pain in adults, multiple guidelines and reviews recommend plain radiography (AP
pelvis and lateral of the symptomatic hip) as the first-line study to assess for
joint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis/cysts, and to exclude
alternative bony pathology. Cross-sectional imaging (MRI or CT) is reserved for
nondiagnostic radiographs or specific alternate concerns (eg, occult fracture,
osteonecrosis, tumor, infection).[1][2][3][4][5][6][7] Bone scan and skeletal survey
are not appropriate initial tests in this scenario.[2][3][8][9][10]

osteoarthritis that plain x-ray can detect and that are relevant to this patient’s
presentation.[4]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 19-year-old man undergoes splenectomy because of crush injuries
sustained in a motor vehicle collision. During the operation, two small
accessory spleens are seen adjacent to the ruptured spleen. The most likely
origin of this patient's congenital anomaly is thickening of an embryologic
layer at which of the following sites?

options.B: Along the lesser curvature of the stomach
options.C: At the origin of the celiac vessels
options.D: In a portion of the hepatic diverticulum

explanation:
Accessory spleens result from supernumerary condensations of mesenchyme
where the spleen normally arises. The spleen is a mesodermal derivative that
develops as a condensation within the dorsal mesogastrium; accessory spleens
arise when splenic tissue buds within this region fail to coalesce with the main
primordium and can be found near the hilum, pancreatic tail, or along the gastric
curvature along the splenic migratory path.[1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8][9]
Therefore, the most likely embryologic origin is a thickening of mesodermal
mesenchyme within the dorsal mesogastrium: (E) Within the dorsal
mesogastrium.[1][2][4][5]
references: Morphogenesis of the Spleen During the Human Embryonic Period. Endo
Accessory Spleens at Autopsy. Unver Dogan N, Uysal II, Demirci S, Dogan

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 27-year-old primigravid woman at 21 weeks’ gestation comes to the
emergency department because of a 2-day
history of moderate headache, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting,
muscle aches, and malaise. She also has had
cough occasionally productive of sputum. Pregnancy had been
uncomplicated. She has no history of serious illness,
and her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. She immigrated to the USA
from India 6 months ago. Temperature is
38.3°C (100.9°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood
pressure is 100/60 mm Hg. On examination,
breath sounds are decreased at the right lung base. Fundal height is 22 cm.
Fetal heart rate is 160/min. Chest x-ray
shows right-sided interstitial infiltrates. Which of the following is the most
likely infectious agent?
explanation:
Influenza A virus
Key reasoning:
Presentation is acute (2 days) with systemic symptoms (fever, myalgias,
headache, malaise, nausea/vomiting) plus cough and tachycardia, which is
typical for influenza-associated community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in
otherwise healthy young adults; chest radiograph often shows interstitial
infiltrates.[1][2][3]
Pregnancy increases risk for severe influenza and lower respiratory tract
involvement due to physiologic and immunologic changes; influenza A/B is
a leading viral etiology of CAP, and viral etiologies are common in younger
outpatients.[1][4][5][6]
Epidemiology in pregnancy supports influenza as a major cause of acute
respiratory illness with lower respiratory tract involvement; studies in
pregnant cohorts show influenza frequently identified among viral ALRTIs.
The ACOG and the Infectious Diseases Society of America emphasize
prompt recognition and treatment of suspected influenza in pregnancy.[3]
[5][6][7][8]
Less likely alternatives:
Streptococcus pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenzae typically cause
lobar or bronchopneumonia rather than predominant interstitial patterns
and lack prominent systemic myalgias; onset can be less abruptly systemic.
[1][2][4]
Mycobacterium tuberculosis usually presents subacutely/chronic, often
with constitutional symptoms over weeks and upper-lobe involvement;
acute myalgias and abrupt symptom onset are atypical.[1]
Legionella pneumophila would suggest high fevers, GI symptoms,
hyponatremia, and exposure risk; not evident here.[1]
Pseudomonas aeruginosa is uncommon without structural lung disease or
healthcare exposure.[1]
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
Clinical implication: In pregnancy with suspected influenza, initiate
neuraminidase inhibitor therapy promptly; oseltamivir 75 mg orally twice daily
for 5 days is recommended by the Infectious Diseases Society of America and
ACOG, ideally within 48 hours but not withholding if later.[6][8]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 2-stage screening program for ovarian cancer is being designed. Which
of the following sets of sensitivity and specificity is
most likely preferred for Stage 1 and Stage 2 of the program?

options.A: Stage 1: High Sensitivity, Low Specificity; Stage 2: High Sensitivity, Low
options.B: Stage 1: High Sensitivity, Low Specificity; Stage 2: Low Sensitivity, High Specificity:
options.C: Stage 1: Low Sensitivity, High Specificity; Stage 2: High Sensitivity, Low
options.D: Stage 1: Low Sensitivity, High Specificity; Stage 2: Low Sensitivity, Low

explanation:
(B)
Rationale:
In two-stage screening, the first-stage test should cast a wide net to
minimize missed cases, prioritizing high sensitivity even at the expense of
• 
specificity. For ovarian cancer, stage shift and mortality benefit depend on
capturing early disease; biomarker-led first steps (for example, CA125 or
multi-marker panels interpreted longitudinally) are designed to be sensitive
screens that trigger second-stage evaluation.[1][2][3][4]
The second-stage test should be highly specific to limit false positives and
unnecessary invasive procedures. In ovarian cancer programs,
confirmatory transvaginal sonography and specialist assessment are
employed to achieve extremely high specificity and acceptable PPV, as
demonstrated by ROCA-based two-step strategies with very high
specificity and low downstream intervention rates. The American College of
Radiology notes that such two-step designs achieve specificity near 99.9%
with few false positives when TVS is reserved for screen-positive
individuals.[1][3][4][5][6]
This configuration—Stage 1 high sensitivity/low specificity followed by Stage 2
low sensitivity/high specificity—best balances early detection with minimization
of harm, consistent with contemporary trial data and methodological principles
for two-stage screening in ovarian cancer.[1][2][3][4][5][6]
references: Normal Risk Ovarian Screening Study: 21-Year Update. Han CY, Lu KH,

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 48-year-old man comes to the physician requesting treatment for alcohol
withdrawal. He reports a 30-year history of consuming 6 to 10 beers daily.
He has had two citations for driving while intoxicated. He has previously
experienced alcohol-associated seizures and withdrawal symptoms. His
vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows palmar
erythema. The most appropriate pharmacotherapy in this patient most
likely has which of the following mechanisms?

options.C: Enhancement of the effect of postsynaptic γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA)

explanation:
The patient is at high risk for alcohol withdrawal seizures; first-line
pharmacotherapy is a benzodiazepine, which mitigates withdrawal by restoring
inhibitory tone. The key mechanism is: Enhancement of the effect of
postsynaptic gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA). Benzodiazepines are the
mainstay of treatment for alcohol withdrawal and reduce seizure and delirium
risk by positive allosteric modulation of the GABA-A receptor, counteracting the
hyperglutamatergic, hypoGABAergic state of withdrawal.[1][2][3][4][5] In those
with liver disease, short-acting agents without active metabolites (lorazepam,
oxazepam) are preferred, though the therapeutic mechanism remains GABA-A
potentiation.[1][2][6]
Dosing context (if initiating treatment): symptom-triggered regimens with
lorazepam 1–2 mg orally or IV every 1–2 hours as needed until symptoms are
minimal, then tapering, are commonly used; diazepam or chlordiazepoxide can
be used when hepatic function is preserved.[1][2][3]
The following table summarizes benzodiazepine choices in alcohol withdrawal
and highlights the class’s role as first-line therapy via GABA-A enhancement,
with liver disease considerations:
This table supports the selection of a benzodiazepine (mechanism: postsynaptic
GABA-A enhancement) and the preference for lorazepam/oxazepam when
hepatic synthetic function is impaired.
Table 1. Summary of Pharmacologic Treatment Recommendations for Patients with the Alcohol
Withdrawal Syndrome and Liver Disease.
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
Rationale against other options:
Blockade of dopamine receptors, decreased dopamine transporter activity,
inhibition of serotonin reuptake, or stimulation of 5-HT receptors target
craving/psychosis/mood, not acute withdrawal seizure prevention.
Increased GABA transaminase activity would lower GABA.
Inhibition of glutamate release/opening glutamate channels is not first-line
for acute withdrawal; NMDA modulation (e.g., acamprosate) is for relapse
prevention post-detox.[2][7]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 7-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 1-year history of
poor performance in school. His parents
say that he is bright, has many friends, and seems to want to do well in
school. His teachers report that he seems
frustrated with his own progress and his inability to meet the expectations
of his parents. Speech fluency and
articulation and motor skills are appropriate for age. Physical examination
shows no abnormalities. When asked to
read during the examination, he has significant difficulty sounding out
words he is unfamiliar with. Visual acuity
test and audiometry show no abnormalities. Which of the following is the
most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
The most likely diagnosis is Learning disorder.
The child has persistent, specific reading impairment—difficulty sounding out
unfamiliar words—despite normal speech, motor skills, and sensory function,
and with otherwise intact cognition and sociability. This pattern is characteristic
of specific learning disorder with impairment in reading (dyslexia), which
requires academic skills substantially below age expectations, persists despite
targeted help, begins in school years, and is not better explained by intellectual
disability, language disorder, or sensory deficits.[1] Expressive language disorder
would present with impaired expressive language (e.g., syntax, verb use), not
isolated decoding failure.[2] Intellectual developmental disorder requires global
deficits in intellectual and adaptive functioning, which are absent here.[3] ADHD
is not suggested by attention/hyperactivity symptoms. Social anxiety disorder
centers on fear of scrutiny and avoidance, not isolated phonologic decoding
deficits.[4] The course is chronic and non-urgent.[5]
To contextualize why social anxiety is unlikely in this vignette (despite poor
school performance), the DSM-5 criteria emphasize fear of negative evaluation
and avoidance of social or performance situations, which are not described in
this case.[4]
Table 1. Diagnostic Criteria for Social Anxiety Disorder.
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
Key features supporting specific learning disorder (reading/dyslexia):
Persistent inaccurate/effortful word reading and difficulty sounding out
words.[1]
Academic underachievement confined to reading; normal speech fluency/
articulation and normal hearing/vision.[1][2]
No global intellectual/adaptive deficits.[3]
Not better accounted for by anxiety or attentional disorders.[1][4]
The American Psychiatric Association’s DSM-5-TR criteria (2022) specify these
diagnostic elements and exclusion criteria.[1]
• 
• 
• 
•

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 35-year-old woman comes to the office for follow-up of chronic pelvic
pain. The patient has had deep pelvic pain since
undergoing total abdominal hysterectomy and radiation therapy for
treatment of cervical cancer 5 years ago. She reports constant,
severe aching pain; she rates the pain at its maximum as a 10 on a 10‐point
scale. The pain worsens during intercourse. All
subsequent cancer follow-up has been negative. Medical history otherwise
is unremarkable. The patient has taken oxycodone for
her pain for 3 years, but she now reports that the oxycodone only partially
relieves her pain. She requests either an increased dose of
oxycodone or a change in her prescription to controlled-release morphine,
which was recently given to her by a friend. She has
smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for the past 15 years. She does not
drink alcoholic beverages. BMI is 18 kg/m². She appears
lethargic. She is oriented to person, place, and time but speaks slowly with
slurred speech. Vital signs are normal. Examination of
the skin shows no abnormalities. Pupils are pinpoint and react sluggishly.
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Inform the patient that she must find a new physician;
options.B: Maintain the dose of oxycodone and add naproxen
options.D: Report the patient's unauthorized drug use to the police

explanation:
The most appropriate next step is to order a urine toxicology screening (option
C) to assess for opioid misuse and ensure patient safety before making any
changes to her pain management, as supported by current guidelines and best
practices.
This patient presents with signs consistent with opioid intoxication (lethargy,
slurred speech, miosis) and reports nonmedical use of controlled-release
morphine. The CDC (United States Centers for Disease Control and Prevention)
recommends reassessing benefits/harms at follow-up and evaluating for misuse
when early warning signs such as sedation or slurred speech are present; urine
testing, pill counts, and PDMP checks are reasonable components of this
assessment.[1][2] ASCO emphasizes structured responses to aberrant behaviors,
beginning with reassessment and adherence/toxicology monitoring, rather than
dose escalation or dismissal.[3] Escalating or switching opioids now (options B/
E) is inappropriate and unsafe. Dismissing from care (A) is discouraged;
suspected opioid use disorder warrants more care, not less.[2][4] Reporting to
law enforcement (D) is generally not appropriate given confidentiality
constraints absent specific legal obligations.[5][6]
After urine toxicology and reassessment, management should include: safety
planning (overdose education, consider naloxone), risk stratification and
monitoring (PDMP, pill counts), optimizing nonopioid and nonpharmacologic
therapies, and evaluation for opioid use disorder with consideration of addiction
or pain specialty referral; buprenorphine may be suitable if OUD is diagnosed
and analgesia is needed.[1][3][4][7]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 48-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus comes to the physician
for a follow-up examination. Current medications are metformin and once-
daily insulin. She travels frequently and works long hours. She says that her
meals are usually fast food. She leads a sedentary lifestyle. She often
forgets to measure her blood glucose concentration. Her last hemoglobin
A1c was measured as 8.4%. Which of the following is the most appropriate
action by the physician to help this patient improve her diabetic control?

options.A: Create an exercise regimen for the patient
options.E: Measure the patient's blood glucose concentration

explanation:
The patient’s suboptimal control is accompanied by clear lifestyle and adherence
barriers (frequent travel, fast food, sedentary behavior, inconsistent SMBG). The
most effective first step is a patient-centered conversation to understand and
address these barriers before adjusting pharmacotherapy or prescribing generic
lifestyle plans. Interventions such as motivational interviewing and structured
diabetes self-management education improve adherence and modestly lower
A1c, and are recommended by the American Diabetes Association and other
societies; premature insulin or metformin dose escalation without addressing
adherence is less effective and may increase treatment burden.[1][2][3][4][5][6]
Therefore, the most appropriate action is to engage the patient to identify
obstacles to dietary adherence and collaboratively develop feasible strategies,
which can then guide targeted referrals (e.g., nutritionist/DSMES) and any
subsequent medication changes.
Answer: (B) Explore barriers to dietary adherence

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 23-year-old woman who is an active-duty enlisted airman in the US Air
Force comes to the base clinic because of
a 4-week history of an itchy sensation in her right ear. She has not had
ringing in her ears or hearing loss. She works
as a fuel specialist and at first attributed her symptom to the earplugs she
uses when she is on the flight line. The
symptom has not improved despite the patient's wearing headphones
instead of the earplugs during the past 2 weeks.
She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs
are within normal limits. During
examination, pulling of the right pinna inferiorly to examine the ear canal
produces pain. The right ear canal appears
erythematous and edematous. The tympanic membrane cannot be fully
visualized because of the presence of
cerumen. Examination of the left ear shows no abnormalities. Which of the
following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Otitis externa (C)
The most likely diagnosis is otitis externa because the patient’s symptoms and
examination findings—especially pain with pinna movement and canal
inflammation—are classic for this condition and are best explained by infection
of the external auditory canal rather than cerumen impaction, trauma, or
dermatologic disease.[1][2][3][4][5] Pruritus, canal erythema/edema, and marked
tenderness with manipulation of the pinna or tragus are hallmark features;
occupational earplug use and minor canal trauma are common precipitants.[1][2]
[3][5][6][7] Cerumen impaction alone would not cause pain with pinna
movement or diffuse canal edema; trauma typically requires a clear inciting
injury with focal findings; polychondritis involves the cartilaginous pinna sparing
the lobule, often bilateral and systemic; seborrheic dermatitis causes scaling/
erythema without severe tragal/pinna tenderness or canal edema.[2][3][4][8]
The diagnosis is clinical; absence of systemic symptoms and unilateral canal
inflammation further support uncomplicated acute otitis externa in this setting.
[1][2][3][4]

A 35-year-old woman comes to the office for follow-up of chronic pelvic
pain. The patient has had deep pelvic pain since
undergoing total abdominal hysterectomy and radiation therapy for
treatment of cervical cancer 5 years ago. She reports constant,
severe aching pain; she rates the pain at its maximum as a 10 on a 10‐point
scale. The pain worsens during intercourse. All
subsequent cancer follow-up has been negative. Medical history otherwise
is unremarkable. The patient has taken oxycodone for
her pain for 3 years, but she now reports that the oxycodone only partially
relieves her pain. She requests either an increased dose of
oxycodone or a change in her prescription to controlled-release morphine,
which was recently given to her by a friend. She has
smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for the past 15 years. She does not
drink alcoholic beverages. BMI is 18 kg/m2. She appears
lethargic. She is oriented to person, place, and time but speaks slowly with
slurred speech. Vital signs are normal. Examination of
the skin shows no abnormalities. Pupils are pinpoint and react sluggishly.
Urine toxicology screening is positive for diazepam, methadone, and
oxycodone. The patient returns to the office 1 week later
to discuss these results. Which of the following is the most appropriate
opening remark?

Option (A) is correct because it opens with nonjudgmental concern, frames the
abnormal toxicology as a safety issue, and immediately orients to evidence-
based treatment for a likely opioid use disorder, consistent with best practice.

and Prevention) emphasizes assessing for opioid use disorder with DSM-5
criteria, avoiding dismissal from care, and offering/arranging medications for
opioid use disorder and harm reduction, not punitive actions.[1][2] NEJM reviews
similarly advise against dismissal and endorse engagement in treatment,
monitoring, and safety planning; a supportive tone improves disclosure and
outcomes.[3][4] Expert consensus on urine drug monitoring recommends
nonjudgmental, nonpunitive communication, eliciting the patient’s perspective,
and collaboratively planning next steps when unexpected results arise.[5] ASCO
guidance for cancer survivors with aberrant drug-related behaviors stresses
reassessment, considering addiction, and involving addiction specialists rather
than immediate termination or legal threats.[6] Motivational interviewing
principles—expressing empathy, supporting autonomy, and eliciting change talk
—align with (A) and improve engagement.[7]

(B) Dismissal is discouraged; it increases harm and misses an opportunity to
initiate life-saving care.[1][2][3]

treatment engagement; details can be explored after establishing safety and
support.[5][7]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 19-year-old man who is a college freshman comes to the office because
of a 4-day history of tender, swollen glands. He also has a 6-day history of
fever, malaise, and decreased appetite. His temperature is 38.7°C (101.7°F).
Physical examination shows swelling of the parotid glands. Which of the
following infectious agents is the most likely cause of these findings?
explanation:
The most likely cause is Mumps virus (D).
Rationale:
Acute, tender parotid swelling (parotitis) with several days of fever, malaise,
and anorexia is the classic presentation of mumps, particularly in
adolescents/young adults and in congregate settings such as college
• 
campuses. The CDC describes parotitis as the hallmark, typically lasting ~5
days and preceded by nonspecific prodrome.[1][2][3][4][5]
EBV infectious mononucleosis more typically presents with fever,
pharyngitis, and posterior cervical lymphadenopathy; parotid swelling is
uncommon, though reported, making EBV less likely here.[6][7][8][9][10][11]
Measles and rubella characteristically present with rash and do not cause
isolated parotitis; hepatitis B does not present with parotid swelling.
Although eliminated in the U.S., mumps outbreaks still occur among college
students, including those previously vaccinated, making clinical suspicion
appropriate in this demographic.[2][3][4][5][12]
Diagnostic note: While the clinical picture suffices for this multiple-choice
question, laboratory confirmation is recommended for public health. The
Infectious Diseases Society of America and the American Society for
Microbiology note that diagnosis can be supported by anti-mumps IgM,
seroconversion/4-fold IgG rise, and/or RT-PCR from a buccal/parotid duct
swab obtained soon after onset; prior vaccination may blunt IgM responses.[12]

A 34-year-old woman who is on active duty in the US Army comes to the
military urgent care clinic because of a 4-
day history of headache, purulent nasal discharge, and left facial pain. She
has not had fever or chills. She has
chronic bilateral knee pain, which she attributes to multiple parachute
jumps while she was assigned to an airborne
unit 7 years ago. The knee pain is exacerbated by long-distance running on
pavement. Her only medication is
occasional ibuprofen for the knee pain. The patient has no known
medication allergies. Temperature is 37.1°C
(98.8°F), pulse is 60/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is
122/80 mm Hg. Cervical lymph nodes are
not palpable. There is tenderness to palpation over the left maxillary sinus.
Nasal examination shows inflamed
turbinates and purulent discharge. The patient needs to blow her nose
twice during the examination. Bilateral
otoscopic examination discloses no abnormalities. Examination of the
oropharynx shows scant purulent discharge
posteriorly. The remainder of the examination discloses no abnormalities.
Which of the following is the most
appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Gram stain and culture of nasal discharge

explanation:
The most appropriate next step is symptomatic management with analgesics and
decongestants for uncomplicated acute rhinosinusitis of 4 days’ duration.
Therefore, the correct answer is: Ibuprofen and decongestant therapy.
Rationale:
Presentation is consistent with viral acute rhinosinusitis (<7–10 days,
nonsevere, no “double sickening”), for which watchful waiting and
symptomatic therapy are recommended; antibiotics are reserved for
persistent (>10 days), severe, or worsening illness.[1][2][3][4]
Imaging (CT or X-ray) and routine Gram stain/culture of nasal discharge
are not indicated in uncomplicated cases.[2][3][5][6]
Trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole is not recommended empirically due to
high resistance in S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae (IDSA).[7]
Practical symptomatic options:
Ibuprofen as needed for pain; consider intranasal corticosteroids and brief
topical nasal decongestant use (≤3–5 days) or oral decongestants for
congestion; saline irrigation may help with purulent drainage.[1][2][4][8]
If symptoms persist beyond 7–10 days or worsen, initiate antibiotics (e.g.,
amoxicillin or amoxicillin–clavulanate per guideline).[1][2][4]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 24-year-old woman, gravida 1, para 1, comes to the office because she and
her husband have been trying to conceive for
the past year without success. She gave birth to her first child at age 18
years by cesarean delivery following fetal distress.
She then developed postpartum endometritis that resolved with
intravenous antibiotic therapy. She used an intrauterine
device (IUD) for 1 year following her first pregnancy but had the device
removed because of intermenstrual bleeding.
Medical history is also remarkable for chlamydial cervicitis treated with
antibiotic therapy as an outpatient at age 21 years,
and recurrent bacterial vaginosis that had been treated with metronidazole
therapy during her pregnancy and on two other
occasions. Vital signs today are within normal limits. Breast and pelvic
examinations disclose no abnormalities.
Hysterosalpingography shows bilateral hydrosalpinx. Which of the
following factors in this patient's history most increased
her risk for development of her condition?
explanation:
The historical factor that most increased this patient’s risk of bilateral
hydrosalpinx is prior sexually transmitted chlamydial infection. Chlamydia
trachomatis commonly ascends from the cervix to the upper genital tract,
causing PID, salpingitis, and subsequent tubal scarring that predisposes to
hydrosalpinx and tubal factor infertility; risks increase with repeat infection and
are stronger than for BV, postpartum endometritis, cesarean delivery, or IUD use.
[1][2][3][4] BV is associated with incident PID but is a weaker and less specific
driver of tubal damage than chlamydia.[2][5][6] IUD-associated PID risk is largely
limited to the first 20 days post-insertion and long-term tubal factor infertility
risk from IUDs is low.[7][8] Postpartum endometritis primarily involves the
endometrium rather than the tubes; its linkage to hydrosalpinx is less robust
than that of chlamydia.[9][10]
Answer: Bolded below.
The correct choice: Chlamydial cervicitis

confers risk in this case.[1]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 38-year-old woman who is a veteran comes to the emergency
department because of a 4-hour history of a sensation of tightness in her
throat and swelling of her face, lips, and tongue. She has not had itching.
During the past 3 weeks, she has had two similar, milder episodes that
resolved without treatment within 24 to 72 hours. Six weeks ago, she began
treatment with an ACE inhibitor for hypertension. She has seasonal
allergies. Her temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations
are 20/min, and blood pressure is 138/81 mm Hg. Physical examination
shows angioedema of the lips and tongue. There is no evidence of urticaria.
Which of the following is the most likely cause of the angioedema in this
patient?
explanation:
The clinical picture is ACE inhibitor–associated, nonpruritic angioedema of the
lips/tongue without urticaria, typically mediated by impaired degradation of
bradykinin early after ACE-I initiation. Therefore, the most likely cause is: 
Bradykinin.
According to a review published in The New England Journal of Medicine:
"ACE inhibitors are thought to cause angioedema through an effect on
the kallikrein–kinin system that reduces the catabolism of bradykinin
and increases its activity, leading to the inflammatory effect seen in
angioedema."
—Antoine Eskander, M.D. et al., University of Toronto and other
institutions. Acute Upper Airway Obstruction. N Engl J Med. November
14, 2019.
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
Key points supporting bradykinin mediation:
Temporal relationship with ACE inhibitor initiation; swelling is nonpitting,
nonpruritic, lacks urticaria, and prominently involves face/tongue.[1][2][3]
[4][5]
Histamine-mediated angioedema typically features urticaria/pruritus and
responds to antihistamines/steroids, unlike ACE-I angioedema.[2][4][5]
Bradykinin increases vascular permeability via B2 receptors; ACE inhibition
reduces bradykinin catabolism.[2][3][5]
In practice:
Discontinue the ACE inhibitor; anticipate possible recurrence for weeks
after cessation.[4][6]
Standard antihistamines/corticosteroids are often ineffective; airway
assessment is paramount. For severe/laryngeal involvement, bradykinin-
targeted agents (e.g., icatibant 30 mg SC; repeat every ≥6 h up to 3 doses in
24 h if needed) can be considered based on available evidence and guideline
discussions.[1][2][5][7]

summarizes angioedema subtypes and laboratory patterns, emphasizing that

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 16-year-old boy with VATER syndrome is brought to the physician
because of 2-week history of harsh cough that
began with a common cold. He receives no medications. Temperature is
37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 70/min, respirations
are 20/min, and blood pressure is 112/64 mm Hg. Examination shows a
harsh vibratory sound in the mid anterior
chest with forced expiration. A systolic murmur is heard best at the lower
left sternal border. There is mild scoliosis.
Spirometry shows an FVC of 2.54 L (78% of predicted) and a slow vital
capacity of 2.88 L (85% of predicted). A
flow volume loop shows scooping on the expiratory limb. Which of the
following is the most likely cause of the
discrepancy between this patient’s forced and slow vital capacity
measurements?
explanation:
Tracheal collapse
The clinical context (history of TEF in VATER association, harsh expiratory
vibratory sound) and spirometry pattern (expiratory scooping on the flow–
volume loop) point to intrathoracic tracheomalacia with dynamic expiratory
collapse. During a forced maneuver, dynamic central airway collapse and early
airway closure reduce exhaled volume, so FVC underestimates true vital
capacity, whereas SVC obtained with a slow maneuver is less affected and closer
to the actual VC. This produces the observed FVC < SVC discrepancy. The
American Thoracic Society/European Respiratory Society technical standards
recognize that dynamic airway collapsibility can lead to a lower FVC than SVC
and that inspiratory–expiratory loop abnormalities help localize intrathoracic
central airway obstruction.[1] Tracheomalacia is classically associated with TEF/
EA and VACTERL, and presents with barking/harsh cough and expiratory noise;
expiratory flow–volume “scooping” and dynamic collapse are well described in
pediatric tracheobronchomalacia.[2][3][4][5]
Alternatives are less consistent:
Bronchospasm primarily causes airflow limitation but does not typically
explain a marked SVC > FVC difference attributable to dynamic central
airway collapse.[6][7][8]
Laryngeal braking and extrathoracic lesions (subglottic mass, upper airway
compression) produce inspiratory plateaus or variable extrathoracic
patterns rather than intrathoracic expiratory collapse with scooped
expiratory limb.[1]
Thus, dynamic intrathoracic airway collapse best explains the discrepant FVC
and SVC in this patient.[1][2][3][4][5]
